body_txt: In the world of business, competition in the form of rivals is an inevitability. Ideally, everyone would compete fairly by using only their own skill and merit. Unfortunately, some people have little to no qualms about sabotaging rival businesses to gain an edge. Said tactics are typically born out of fear of competition and a desire for greater profits. Said saboteurs might be powerful businesses trying to maintain their power, new players trying to take out or weaken the old guard, or moderately successful businesses that want to create a monopoly. The tactics used often depend on the nature of the business involved. A farmer might sabotage other farmers by destroying or poisoning their crops and livestock. If a business is dependent on supply chains, a rival might sabotage the chain by stealing, destroying, or sabotaging the supplies. Restaurants might steal recipes or poison the food supplies. Certain tactics can work on any kind of business. False lawsuits can be used to financially harm a rival and harm their reputation. Slander and libel can hurt a rival's reputation and drive customers and clients away. Destroying or damaging a business's site can either make the business harder to run or shut the business down outright. Hurting or even killing the employees can also be an effective way to hurt a business. A type of act frequently committed by the Corrupt Corporate Executive , the Evil, Inc. , and the Evil Capitalism side of Good Capitalism, Evil Capitalism . Rarely done by the Honest Corporate Executive (even for taking down a corrupt business). See also Corporate Warfare , in which businesses wage outright war with one another. Compare Removing the Rival , where someone uses underhanded means to deal with a rival, but it isn't specifically about being business competitors. While this sort of thing does sometimes happen in real life , due to being a morality-related trope, No Real Life Examples, Please!
